The next principle of persuasion for us to explore is the principle of consistency. The last two principles we've covered, social proof and authority, work by giving people evidence of what those around them, peers or experts, have said or done. Consistency, on the other hand, works by giving people evidence of what they themselves have said or done. In other words, what prior commitments they have made. Because people feel the need to be consistent with their commitments, you'll significantly improve the odds that they will accept your recommendations by showing them that it is aligned with their prior statements, positions and actions. Recall that in Dr. Nidard's model, there are three distinct sets of principles that increase your influence. The first set, reciprocation, liking and unity, give you greater persuasive traction by deepening the relationships that others feel with you. The second set, social proof and authority, do so by reducing others' uncertainty about the value of your offer. The final set, consistency and scarcity, make your appeals more successful by moving others to comply behaviorally with your request or proposal, to physically act on it. Let's focus on the consistency principle first.
